mnemonics.title: Project Characteristics Memory Aid
mnemonics.content: TUP - Temporary (definite end), Unique (novel deliverable), Progressive (elaborated through iterations)

mnemonics.title: Iron Triangle Components
mnemonics.content: STC - Scope, Time, Cost - remember 'STC: Scope Takes Care' - balancing these three ensures project care

mnemonics.title: PMBOK Process Groups Order
mnemonics.content: IPEMC - Initiating, Planning, Executing, Monitoring & Controlling, Closing - remember 'I Plan Everything My Company' or 'I Pee In The Pot, Then Pee In The Pot, Then Pee In The Pot, Then Pee In The Pot, Then Pee In The Pot, Then Pee In The Pot, Then Close' - 'Initiating, Planning, Executing, Monitoring, Controlling, Closing' - 'I Prefer Eating My Cookies'

mnemonics.title: Top CHAOS Report Failure Factors
mnemonics.content: URE - Unrealistic Expectations, Requirements Change, Executive Support missing - remember 'Users Really Exasperate' or 'URE = Unhappy Results Everywhere'

acronyms.usage: Standard guide published by PMI defining project management best practices, process groups, and knowledge areas
UK government-developed methodology emphasizing controlled, process-based project management with defined roles and stages
Global professional organization for project managers, offers PMP certification and PMBOK standards
Individual with deep expertise in a specific domain (e.g., banking, healthcare) who provides requirements input
Hierarchical decomposition of total project scope into manageable work packages
Document issued to vendors inviting bids for software development projects
Contract defining expected service delivery standards between provider and client

visualTips.visualization: Imagine an equilateral triangle with 'Scope' on the left, 'Time' on the right, and 'Cost' at the top. The interior represents 'Quality'. Pushing any side inward (e.g., increasing scope) pushes another side outward (more time/cost). Visualize it as a balloon—squeeze one area, another expands.

Draw a horizontal timeline: Initiating (small circle at start) → Planning (growing wedge) → Executing (largest wedge, core work) → Monitoring/Controlling (parallel to Executing) → Closing (tapering wedge at end). Show feedback arrows from Monitoring back to earlier phases.

Place the project in the center. Draw concentric circles: Inner circle = key decision makers (Sponsor, Customer); Middle circle = directly impacted (End Users, Dev Team); Outer circle = indirectly affected (QA, Maintenance). Draw power/interest grid - high power/high interest requires closest management.

analogies.analogy: Like drawing a sketch: initial rough outline (concept), then adding details line by line (iteration), with each iteration refining and improving based on feedback until the final detailed drawing emerges. You cannot create a perfect detailed drawing in one attempt—refinement is continuous.

Think of a three-legged stool. If you cut one leg shorter (reduce time), the stool becomes unstable unless you adjust the other legs (increase cost or reduce scope). You cannot change one leg in isolation without affecting stability.

Like borrowing money from a loan shark—taking shortcuts now (quick and dirty code) seems to save time, but the interest (rework, bugs, maintenance difficulty) compounds until the debt becomes overwhelming. Eventually, you must 'pay back' the debt through refactoring or suffer project instability.

A project is like planning and executing a wedding (temporary, unique, has definite end). Operations are like ongoing marriage maintenance (continuous, repetitive activities). After the wedding (project ends), you transition to ongoing marriage (operations).
